narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 60.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 35, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings, family income level, and planned full-time enrollment status. Among the factors considered, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 stood out as the strongest influence pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, as it placed you well below the average of around 6 for students who passed. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed notably in the same direction, again lower than the typical 6 seen among successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided the key counterbalance, pulling the prediction toward success since your 3.40 exceeds the 3.24 average for those who passed. Compared to students who were admitted—mostly full-time enrollees with stronger early rankings and balanced test scores—your profile showed these early academic deciles as the biggest gaps, even with your solid GPA and high family income aligning more closely with passers. While your LSAT was a bit under the 37 average for successful students, it wasn't the main driver here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven signal based on patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and focus on strategies to improve your class rankings in future coursework, perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Many applicants in similar positions strengthen their profiles this way and reapply successfully. You might also explore part-time programs or gap-year experiences that highlight your resilience. We're here to support your next steps—keep pushing, as your above-average GPA shows you have real strengths to build on.
